ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Hokkaido milk shokupan; closest parent is white bread food product (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Bread, white) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for enriched Japanese milk bread.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (Bread) is the closest parent concept; no specific code for shokupan.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard slice of Hokkaido milk shokupan weighs approximately 40 g, equivalent to roughly one-eighth of a standard 320 g loaf.

nutrient_source.title: Bread, white, commercially prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 325871)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Hokkaido milk shokupan is an enriched Japanese milk bread with higher fat and sugar content than standard white bread due to cream, condensed milk, and butter. USDA SR Legacy white bread (FDC ID 325871) is used as the closest approved base. Values have been adjusted upward for energy, fat, saturated fat, and sugar to reflect the enriched formulation typical of Hokkaido-style shokupan, consistent with published compositional analyses of Japanese milk bread. Protein and carbohydrate are modestly adjusted. All adjustments are conservative and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hokkaido milk shokupan. GI of 71 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread (GI = 71, glucose reference), which is the closest analogous food. Hokkaido shokupan is an enriched white bread; the higher fat and sugar content may marginally lower the effective GI, but 71 is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate consistent with the white bread category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (71 × 20.4) / 100 ≈ 14.5, rounded to 14.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~124 mg per 40 g slice)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one slice and avoid pairing with high-sodium spreads such as salted butter or processed cheese, as cumulative sodium intake can contribute to elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) and significant refined carbohydrate content (~51 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Hokkaido milk shokupan in small portions (one thin slice) and pair it with protein or healthy fat to blunt the postprandial glucose response, given its high GI and substantial refined carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.8 g per 100 g) from butter and cream, combined with moderate cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of Hokkaido milk shokupan due to its saturated fat content from cream and butter, which can contribute to LDL cholesterol elevation when consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hokkaido Milk Shokupan is a premium Japanese-style enriched white bread originating from the Hokkaido region of Japan, renowned for its exceptionally soft, pillowy crumb and pronounced milky sweetness. The bread is made using a combination of two traditional Japanese bread-making techniques: the tangzhong (hot water roux) method, which gelatinises a portion of the flour with hot liquid to lock in moisture and extend shelf life, and the sponge dough method, which develops a complex flavour through a pre-ferment stage. The enriched dough incorporates whole milk, light cream, condensed milk, fine sugar, and unsalted butter, giving the finished loaf a tender, moist texture and a subtly sweet, milky flavour profile that distinguishes it from standard white bread. The crust is thin and golden, while the interior crumb is feathery and pulls apart in soft, layered sheets. Nutritionally, Hokkaido milk shokupan is a calorie-dense refined carbohydrate food (~320 kcal per 100 g) with moderate protein (~8.2 g), elevated fat (~8.5 g, predominantly saturated from dairy), and high sugar (~10.5 g) relative to standard white bread. Its high glycemic index (GI ~71) makes it a food of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The bread is widely sold in Singapore at specialty bakeries and Chinese-style dessert and bread shops, often consumed as a breakfast item, afternoon snack, or paired with kaya, butter, or condensed milk. It is best consumed on the day of purchase to preserve its characteristic soft texture.
common_user_queries: Is shokupan the same as Hokkaido milk bread?
How many calories in Hokkaido milk shokupan?
Is Hokkaido milk bread good for diabetics?
What is the GI of Japanese milk bread?
What makes Hokkaido bread so soft?
How much sugar is in Hokkaido milk toast?
Is shokupan suitable for people with high blood pressure?
meal_context: served plain as a soft bread

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Hokkaido milk shokupan exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy white bread (FDC ID 325871) with upward adjustments for fat, saturated fat, sugar, and energy to reflect the enriched dairy-rich formulation. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database white bread reference (GI 71). Confidence is medium because the adaptation is methodologically defensible but not based on a direct laboratory analysis of this specific product.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 325871 (white bread) with enrichment adjustments for cream, condensed milk, and butter typical of Hokkaido-style shokupan. Energy, fat, saturated fat, sugar, and cholesterol have been adjusted upward; protein and carbohydrate are modestly adjusted. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for white bread and is not reliably estimated for this enriched formulation. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for white bread and cannot be reliably estimated for this product. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for white bread. 5. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate based on white bread from the University of Sydney GI Database; the actual GI of this enriched product may be marginally lower due to fat and sugar content slowing gastric emptying. 6. Halal status is unconfirmed; the product is sold at RUXU 入续 糖水铺与包 (Funan, Singapore) and may contain non-halal ingredients. 7. The input record incorrectly classified this food under cuisine: Chinese and food_group: Beverages; corrected to cuisine: Japanese and food_group: Grains and staples based on the food's actual identity. 8. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as white bread and enriched milk bread contain negligible vitamin C, consistent with USDA data.
